questions.question: For destination 10.10.20.9, which route is selected if all listed entries exist?
questions.explanation: Routers use longest-prefix match first. 10.10.20.0/24 via G0/1 is the most specific matching prefix, so it wins over broader routes and the default route.

questions.question: For destination 172.16.31.77, which route is selected if all listed entries exist?
questions.explanation: Routers use longest-prefix match first. 172.16.31.64/26 via G0/2 is the most specific matching prefix, so it wins over broader routes and the default route.

questions.question: For destination 192.168.14.130, which route is selected if all listed entries exist?
questions.explanation: Routers use longest-prefix match first. 192.168.14.128/25 via G0/1 is the most specific matching prefix, so it wins over broader routes and the default route.

questions.question: For destination 198.51.100.67, which route is selected if all listed entries exist?
questions.explanation: Routers use longest-prefix match first. 198.51.100.64/27 via G0/1 is the most specific matching prefix, so it wins over broader routes and the default route.

questions.question: For destination 203.0.113.201, which route is selected if all listed entries exist?
questions.explanation: Routers use longest-prefix match first. 203.0.113.192/27 via G0/2 is the most specific matching prefix, so it wins over broader routes and the default route.

questions.question: For destination 10.44.8.190, which route is selected if all listed entries exist?
questions.explanation: Routers use longest-prefix match first. 10.44.8.128/25 via G0/3 is the most specific matching prefix, so it wins over broader routes and the default route.

questions.question: For destination 172.20.5.15, which route is selected if all listed entries exist?
questions.explanation: Routers use longest-prefix match first. 172.20.5.0/28 via G0/0 is the most specific matching prefix, so it wins over broader routes and the default route.

questions.question: For destination 192.0.2.126, which route is selected if all listed entries exist?
questions.explanation: Routers use longest-prefix match first. 192.0.2.64/26 via G0/1 is the most specific matching prefix, so it wins over broader routes and the default route.

questions.question: For destination 10.120.14.9, which route is selected if all listed entries exist?
questions.explanation: Routers use longest-prefix match first. 10.120.14.0/24 via G0/2 is the most specific matching prefix, so it wins over broader routes and the default route.

questions.question: For destination 172.31.240.34, which route is selected if all listed entries exist?
questions.explanation: Routers use longest-prefix match first. 172.31.240.32/27 via G0/2 is the most specific matching prefix, so it wins over broader routes and the default route.

questions.question: For destination 10.5.1.60, which route is selected if all listed entries exist?
questions.explanation: Routers use longest-prefix match first. 10.5.1.32/27 via G0/2 is the most specific matching prefix, so it wins over broader routes and the default route.

questions.question: For destination 172.18.200.130, which route is selected if all listed entries exist?
questions.explanation: Routers use longest-prefix match first. 172.18.200.128/25 via G0/1 is the most specific matching prefix, so it wins over broader routes and the default route.

questions.question: The prefix 10.50.10.0/24 is learned from multiple sources with same mask. Which route is installed?
questions.explanation: When prefix length is equal, the route with lowest administrative distance is chosen. For this case, static (AD 1) has the best AD.

questions.question: The prefix 172.16.40.0/24 is learned from multiple sources with same mask. Which route is installed?
questions.explanation: When prefix length is equal, the route with lowest administrative distance is chosen. For this case, eBGP (AD 20) has the best AD.

questions.question: The prefix 192.168.70.0/24 is learned from multiple sources with same mask. Which route is installed?
questions.explanation: When prefix length is equal, the route with lowest administrative distance is chosen. For this case, EIGRP internal (AD 90) has the best AD.

questions.question: The prefix 10.10.88.0/24 is learned from multiple sources with same mask. Which route is installed?
questions.explanation: When prefix length is equal, the route with lowest administrative distance is chosen. For this case, static (AD 5) has the best AD.

questions.question: The prefix 172.22.1.0/24 is learned from multiple sources with same mask. Which route is installed?
questions.explanation: When prefix length is equal, the route with lowest administrative distance is chosen. For this case, EIGRP internal (AD 90) has the best AD.

questions.question: The prefix 198.51.100.0/24 is learned from multiple sources with same mask. Which route is installed?
questions.explanation: When prefix length is equal, the route with lowest administrative distance is chosen. For this case, connected (AD 0) has the best AD.

questions.question: The prefix 203.0.113.0/24 is learned from multiple sources with same mask. Which route is installed?
questions.explanation: When prefix length is equal, the route with lowest administrative distance is chosen. For this case, EIGRP internal (AD 90) has the best AD.

questions.question: The prefix 10.77.32.0/24 is learned from multiple sources with same mask. Which route is installed?
questions.explanation: When prefix length is equal, the route with lowest administrative distance is chosen. For this case, OSPF (AD 110) has the best AD.

questions.question: The prefix 172.30.30.0/24 is learned from multiple sources with same mask. Which route is installed?
questions.explanation: When prefix length is equal, the route with lowest administrative distance is chosen. For this case, static (AD 10) has the best AD.

questions.question: The prefix 192.0.2.0/24 is learned from multiple sources with same mask. Which route is installed?
questions.explanation: When prefix length is equal, the route with lowest administrative distance is chosen. For this case, OSPF (AD 110) has the best AD.

questions.question: What does 'S 0.0.0.0/0 [1/0] via 10.1.1.1' indicate in a routing table?
options: A static default route with AD 1 toward next-hop 10.1.1.1
questions.explanation: Code S means static, 0.0.0.0/0 is default, and [1/0] shows AD 1 metric 0. This is the primary static default path.

questions.question: What does route code 'L' represent in show ip route output?
options: A local /32 route for the router interface IP
questions.explanation: Code L is auto-created for each interface IP address so packets to the router's own address are delivered locally.

questions.question: Which command correctly adds a static route to 192.168.50.0 with mask 255.255.255.0 via 10.1.1.2?
questions.explanation: IPv4 static syntax is 'ip route <network> <mask> <next-hop|interface>'. ip route 192.168.50.0 255.255.255.0 10.1.1.2 matches that exact format.

questions.question: Which command correctly adds a static route to 172.20.14.0 with mask 255.255.255.0 via 10.2.2.2?
questions.explanation: IPv4 static syntax is 'ip route <network> <mask> <next-hop|interface>'. ip route 172.20.14.0 255.255.255.0 10.2.2.2 matches that exact format.

questions.question: Which command correctly adds a static route to 10.44.88.0 with mask 255.255.255.0 via 192.0.2.2?
questions.explanation: IPv4 static syntax is 'ip route <network> <mask> <next-hop|interface>'. ip route 10.44.88.0 255.255.255.0 192.0.2.2 matches that exact format.

questions.question: Which command correctly adds a static route to 192.168.120.0 with mask 255.255.255.128 via 10.3.3.2?
questions.explanation: IPv4 static syntax is 'ip route <network> <mask> <next-hop|interface>'. ip route 192.168.120.0 255.255.255.128 10.3.3.2 matches that exact format.

questions.question: Which command correctly adds a static route to 172.31.240.0 with mask 255.255.255.224 via 10.4.4.2?
questions.explanation: IPv4 static syntax is 'ip route <network> <mask> <next-hop|interface>'. ip route 172.31.240.0 255.255.255.224 10.4.4.2 matches that exact format.

questions.question: Which command correctly adds a static route to 198.51.100.0 with mask 255.255.255.0 via 10.5.5.2?
questions.explanation: IPv4 static syntax is 'ip route <network> <mask> <next-hop|interface>'. ip route 198.51.100.0 255.255.255.0 10.5.5.2 matches that exact format.

questions.question: Which command correctly adds a static route to 203.0.113.64 with mask 255.255.255.192 via 10.6.6.2?
questions.explanation: IPv4 static syntax is 'ip route <network> <mask> <next-hop|interface>'. ip route 203.0.113.64 255.255.255.192 10.6.6.2 matches that exact format.

questions.question: Which command correctly adds a static route to 10.200.14.0 with mask 255.255.255.0 via 10.7.7.2?
questions.explanation: IPv4 static syntax is 'ip route <network> <mask> <next-hop|interface>'. ip route 10.200.14.0 255.255.255.0 10.7.7.2 matches that exact format.

questions.question: Which command correctly adds a static route to 172.18.5.128 with mask 255.255.255.192 via 10.8.8.2?
questions.explanation: IPv4 static syntax is 'ip route <network> <mask> <next-hop|interface>'. ip route 172.18.5.128 255.255.255.192 10.8.8.2 matches that exact format.

questions.question: Which command correctly adds a static route to 192.168.77.0 with mask 255.255.255.0 via 10.9.9.2?
questions.explanation: IPv4 static syntax is 'ip route <network> <mask> <next-hop|interface>'. ip route 192.168.77.0 255.255.255.0 10.9.9.2 matches that exact format.

questions.question: Which command configures a floating static route to 10.10.10.0 via 192.0.2.10 with AD 130?
questions.explanation: A floating static route is just a static route with a higher administrative distance value at the end. The correct IOS format is: ip route 10.10.10.0 255.255.255.0 192.0.2.10 130.

questions.question: Which command configures a floating static route to 172.16.88.0 via 192.0.2.14 with AD 150?
questions.explanation: A floating static route is just a static route with a higher administrative distance value at the end. The correct IOS format is: ip route 172.16.88.0 255.255.255.0 192.0.2.14 150.

questions.question: Which command configures a floating static route to 192.168.77.0 via 192.0.2.18 with AD 140?
questions.explanation: A floating static route is just a static route with a higher administrative distance value at the end. The correct IOS format is: ip route 192.168.77.0 255.255.255.0 192.0.2.18 140.

questions.question: Which command configures a floating static route to 10.44.44.0 via 192.0.2.22 with AD 200?
questions.explanation: A floating static route is just a static route with a higher administrative distance value at the end. The correct IOS format is: ip route 10.44.44.0 255.255.255.0 192.0.2.22 200.

questions.question: Which command creates IPv4 default route via 10.10.10.1?
questions.explanation: IPv4 default is 0.0.0.0/0, so static default route must use 0.0.0.0 and 0.0.0.0 as destination and mask.

questions.question: Which command creates a host route to only 192.168.99.77 via 10.1.1.1?
questions.explanation: A host route is /32, so the subnet mask must be 255.255.255.255 to match exactly one IP.

questions.question: How do you remove static route 'ip route 10.10.50.0 255.255.255.0 192.0.2.5'?
questions.explanation: IOS removes configuration by prefixing the exact original line with 'no'.

questions.question: Branch reaches HQ but HQ cannot initiate traffic to branch LAN. Most likely issue?
options: Missing return route from HQ toward branch subnet
questions.explanation: This symptom usually means forward path exists but reverse path does not. Add or advertise the return route.

questions.question: Why use a summary static route to Null0?
options: To discard traffic to unknown sub-prefixes inside the summary and avoid loops
questions.explanation: A summary can attract traffic for subnets that do not exist. Null0 safely drops those unmatched destinations.

questions.question: On multi-access Ethernet, why can next-hop-only static be better than interface-only static?
options: It avoids unnecessary ARP for every destination in that route
questions.explanation: Interface-only static on multi-access can trigger ARP for many hosts. Specifying next-hop improves behavior and clarity.

questions.question: Which command creates an IPv6 static route to 2001:db8:10:10::/64 via 2001:db8:0:1::2?
questions.explanation: IPv6 static route syntax is 'ipv6 route <prefix> <next-hop|interface>'. The correct form here is: ipv6 route 2001:db8:10:10::/64 2001:db8:0:1::2.

questions.question: Which command creates an IPv6 static route to 2001:db8:20:20::/64 via 2001:db8:0:2::2?
questions.explanation: IPv6 static route syntax is 'ipv6 route <prefix> <next-hop|interface>'. The correct form here is: ipv6 route 2001:db8:20:20::/64 2001:db8:0:2::2.

questions.question: Which command creates an IPv6 static route to 2001:db8:30:30::/64 via 2001:db8:0:3::2?
questions.explanation: IPv6 static route syntax is 'ipv6 route <prefix> <next-hop|interface>'. The correct form here is: ipv6 route 2001:db8:30:30::/64 2001:db8:0:3::2.

questions.question: Which command creates an IPv6 static route to 2001:db8:40:40::/64 via 2001:db8:0:4::2?
questions.explanation: IPv6 static route syntax is 'ipv6 route <prefix> <next-hop|interface>'. The correct form here is: ipv6 route 2001:db8:40:40::/64 2001:db8:0:4::2.

questions.question: Which command creates an IPv6 static route to 2001:db8:50:50::/64 via 2001:db8:0:5::2?
questions.explanation: IPv6 static route syntax is 'ipv6 route <prefix> <next-hop|interface>'. The correct form here is: ipv6 route 2001:db8:50:50::/64 2001:db8:0:5::2.

questions.question: Which command creates an IPv6 static route to 2001:db8:60:60::/64 via 2001:db8:0:6::2?
questions.explanation: IPv6 static route syntax is 'ipv6 route <prefix> <next-hop|interface>'. The correct form here is: ipv6 route 2001:db8:60:60::/64 2001:db8:0:6::2.

questions.question: Which command creates an IPv6 static route to 2001:db8:70:70::/64 via 2001:db8:0:7::2?
questions.explanation: IPv6 static route syntax is 'ipv6 route <prefix> <next-hop|interface>'. The correct form here is: ipv6 route 2001:db8:70:70::/64 2001:db8:0:7::2.

questions.question: Which command creates an IPv6 static route to 2001:db8:80:80::/64 via 2001:db8:0:8::2?
questions.explanation: IPv6 static route syntax is 'ipv6 route <prefix> <next-hop|interface>'. The correct form here is: ipv6 route 2001:db8:80:80::/64 2001:db8:0:8::2.

questions.question: In CCNA single-area OSPF, what area is used?
questions.explanation: Single-area OSPF uses area 0, the backbone area, for all participating links.

questions.question: Which command starts OSPFv2 process 10?
questions.explanation: IOS enters OSPFv2 process mode with 'router ospf <process-id>'.

questions.question: OSPF process ID must match between neighbors.
options: Only on DR links it must match
True: process ID must match exactly
Only in area 0 it must match
False: process ID is locally significant
questions.explanation: Process ID is local to each router. Area, timers, auth, and network parameters determine adjacency.

questions.question: OSPF router ID selection without manual RID uses:
options: Highest loopback IP, else highest active physical IP
questions.explanation: RID order is configured router-id first, then highest loopback, then highest physical interface IP.

questions.question: After changing OSPF router-id, what is typically needed to apply it now?
questions.explanation: Router ID is chosen when the process starts, so you must restart the process (or reboot) to apply new RID immediately.

questions.question: Wildcard for mask 255.255.255.0 is:
questions.explanation: Wildcard is inverse mask. Inverting 255.255.255.0 gives 0.0.0.255.

questions.question: What does passive-interface do in OSPF?
options: Advertises subnet but sends no hellos on that interface
questions.explanation: Passive interface stops neighbor formation on that segment while keeping connected network advertisement.

questions.question: Default OSPF network type on Ethernet is:
questions.explanation: Ethernet defaults to broadcast network type, which uses DR/BDR election.

questions.question: On point-to-point OSPF links:
questions.explanation: Point-to-point links form full adjacency directly and do not use DR/BDR roles.

questions.question: Which OSPF timers must match for adjacency?
questions.explanation: Hello packet checks include hello and dead intervals; mismatch prevents adjacency.

questions.question: Persistent EXSTART in OSPF usually suggests:
questions.explanation: EXSTART/EXCHANGE stuck states commonly indicate MTU mismatch during database description exchange.

questions.question: Route code 'O' in show ip route means:
questions.explanation: In IPv4 routing table, O means OSPF intra-area route.

questions.question: Best command to verify OSPF neighbors and state:
questions.explanation: Neighbor table directly shows FULL/2WAY/EXSTART and peer IDs.

questions.question: On broadcast segment, 2WAY between two DROTHER routers is:
questions.explanation: DROTHER routers form FULL with DR/BDR, often staying 2WAY with each other.

questions.question: OSPF interface cost is derived from:
questions.explanation: Default OSPF metric uses the bandwidth-based cost formula.

questions.question: Why keep reference bandwidth consistent across routers?
options: To keep path cost calculations comparable network-wide
questions.explanation: Different reference values create inconsistent cost math and unpredictable path preference.

questions.question: Command to inject default route into OSPF (when present):
questions.explanation: This command advertises 0.0.0.0/0 into OSPF from an ASBR when default exists.

questions.question: Area mismatch between two OSPF interfaces on same link causes:
questions.explanation: Area ID must match in hello checks; mismatch prevents neighbor formation.

questions.question: Interface-level OSPFv2 enable command:
questions.explanation: Modern IOS supports per-interface OSPFv2 assignment with process and area.

questions.question: DR election on broadcast uses first:
questions.explanation: Election checks priority first; router ID is only tie-breaker.

questions.question: OSPF priority 0 means:
options: Router cannot become DR/BDR on that segment
questions.explanation: Priority 0 makes router ineligible for DR/BDR but still able to form adjacency.

questions.question: Command showing area/timers/cost per OSPF interface:
questions.explanation: This command is the per-interface OSPF troubleshooting staple.

questions.question: Common OSPFv3 area assignment style:
questions.explanation: OSPFv3 is interface-centric on IOS/XE.

questions.question: Global command required for IPv6 forwarding with OSPFv3:
questions.explanation: Without ipv6 unicast-routing, IPv6 control-plane may exist but forwarding is disabled.

questions.question: Why OSPFv3 next-hop often appears as FE80::?
questions.explanation: IPv6 routing protocols commonly exchange hellos over link-local addresses.

questions.question: Command entering OSPFv3 process mode (IOS XE style):
questions.explanation: This is the typical process-level entry point on IOS XE platforms.

questions.question: If OSPF configured but no learned routes appear, first check:
questions.explanation: Route exchange depends on adjacency/LSDB sync. Verify neighbor state before deeper checks.

questions.question: Authentication mismatch on OSPF neighbors leads to:
questions.explanation: Auth type/key mismatch fails hello validation.

questions.question: How can you influence OSPF path choice?
options: Tune interface cost or bandwidth/reference settings
questions.explanation: OSPF selects lowest cumulative cost. Cost parameters are the intended tuning levers.

questions.question: Command giving high-level OSPF running status and advertised networks:
questions.explanation: show ip protocols summarizes dynamic routing process behavior and configured networks.

questions.question: OSPF-enabled interface administratively down means:
questions.explanation: OSPF hellos cannot be sent/received when interface is down.

questions.question: SPF in OSPF refers to:
options: Shortest Path First algorithm run on LSDB
questions.explanation: OSPF computes best paths with Dijkstra SPF using link-state topology.

questions.question: Most direct per-interface mismatch command:
questions.explanation: This view exposes area, auth, timers, network type and state on one interface.

questions.question: Which command confirms route code and next-hop for a default route?
questions.explanation: Querying exact prefix 0.0.0.0 reveals active default route source and next-hop.

questions.question: Which two mismatches commonly prevent OSPF neighbors from reaching FULL state? (Choose two.)
questions.explanation: Area and hello/dead values are mandatory hello checks. Process ID and hostname are local attributes and do not need to match.

questions.question: Which two commands are best to validate OSPF adjacency and learned routes? (Choose two.)
questions.explanation: One command verifies neighbor state, the other verifies OSPF routes in RIB. Together they confirm control-plane and route installation.

questions.question: On broadcast segments, which two factors determine DR election order? (Choose two.)
questions.explanation: DR/BDR election checks highest priority first, then highest router ID if priorities tie.

questions.question: Which two actions legitimately influence OSPF path selection? (Choose two.)
questions.explanation: OSPF path choice is metric-based. Cost and reference bandwidth tuning are valid metric controls.

questions.question: For OSPFv3 adjacency troubleshooting, which two checks are highest priority? (Choose two.)
options: Same OSPF area on both interfaces
questions.explanation: OSPFv3 requires IPv6 routing enabled and matching OSPF interface area configuration.

questions.question: Which two statements are true about floating static routes? (Choose two.)
options: They install only when better route disappears
They use higher AD than primary route
questions.explanation: Floating static is designed as backup by raising AD so primary dynamic/static route remains preferred.

questions.question: Dual-ISP branch failover with static routing is best achieved by which two configs? (Choose two.)
options: Primary default route with lower AD
Secondary default route with higher AD
questions.explanation: Different AD values enforce deterministic primary/backup behavior without protocol complexity.

questions.question: Which two observations suggest missing return route in a routed network? (Choose two.)
options: Initiator reaches remote host but remote cannot initiate back
Both directions fail due to VLAN mismatch
Neighbor table shows FULL on all links
questions.explanation: One-way reachability is classic reverse-path issue. Add route back toward source subnet.

questions.question: Which two IOS commands are directly useful to validate static route configuration and installation? (Choose two.)
questions.explanation: One confirms the configured line, the other confirms RIB installation and next-hop status.

questions.question: Which two statements correctly describe longest-prefix match? (Choose two.)
options: A /32 host route beats a /24 route
Only dynamic routes use prefix comparison
Administrative distance is checked before prefix length
A default route is used only if no longer prefix matches
RIP routes always beat static routes
questions.explanation: Prefix specificity is evaluated first; AD and metric comparisons occur among routes with the same prefix length.

questions.question: If OSPF route to 10.10.10.0/24 disappears and floating static AD 150 exists, what should occur next?
options: Only connected routes can replace dynamic
Floating static route becomes active in RIB
questions.explanation: When primary route is withdrawn, next best valid route (higher AD floating static) is installed automatically.

questions.question: Which command confirms current OSPF router ID in use?
options: show ip ospf | include Router ID
questions.explanation: Process output includes active router ID, which is critical when troubleshooting adjacency and DR election behavior.

questions.question: For destination 2001:db8:100:10::1, which route is most specific?
questions.explanation: Longest-prefix logic applies equally in IPv6. /64 is more specific than /48, /32, or default.

questions.question: A static route is configured but missing from table. Most likely reason?
options: Next-hop or outgoing interface is currently unreachable
questions.explanation: IOS installs static route only if forwarding path can be resolved. Broken next-hop connectivity prevents installation.
